original_abstract:
The selection of standardized stimuli is a critical prerequisite for robust affective research. While established databases for visual, auditory, and lexical materials have significantly advanced the field, researchers frequently encounter limitations such as insufficient stimulus counts within specific categories and outdated image quality. To address these constraints, we introduce the Nencki Affective Picture System (NAPS), a comprehensive database of 1,356 high-resolution, realistic photographs. The images are organized into five distinct semantic categories: people, faces, animals, objects, and landscapes.

Normative affective ratings were gathered from a sample of 204 participants, primarily of European descent, who evaluated each image across the dimensions of valence, arousal, and approach–avoidance using computerized bipolar slider scales. We provide detailed descriptive statistics for these dimensions across all categories and validate our findings through direct comparison with the Self-Assessment Manikin (SAM) and the International Affective Picture System (IAPS). Furthermore, the database includes objective physical parameters for each photograph, such as luminance, contrast, entropy, and color composition in the L*a*b* space. By providing a large-scale, high-quality stimulus set with detailed emotional and physical profiles, NAPS enables researchers to exert greater experimental control and minimize stimulus repetition. The database is available for non-commercial scientific use via the project website.
humanized_abstract:
The selection of standardized stimuli is one of the conditions necessary to conduct affective research in a reliable fashion. While there are several databases of visual, auditory and lexical stimuli that have facilitated the investigation of human affective processing, there are many drawbacks to their use such as a lack of sufficient number of trials for some of the categories and an old quality of the pictures. Therefore, we have recently created a new database of affective stimuli, named the Nencki Affective Picture System (NAPS). The NAPS consists of 1356 high-quality, high-resolution, realistic pictures. Stimuli of all pictures were categorized into five semantic groups: people, faces, animals, objects and landscapes.

We collected normative affective ratings from a cohort of 204 white, predominantly European background individuals. Ratings were elicited to a series of standardized photographs that were assessed for their effects on each of the three dimensions of valence, arousal and approach–avoidance using computerized bipolar slider scales. The database is documented with respect to its descriptive statistics, and the ratings are verified and validated through comparison with ratings from other databases, such as the Self-Assessment Manikin (SAM) and the International Affective Picture System (IAPS). Additional, physical image parameters, including luminance, contrast, entropy and a full colour specification in the L*a*b* space are also recorded. This high quality stimulus set and database of ratings for a large cohort of images provides researchers with much greater control over their experimental procedures, and the need for repeated use of the same images is substantially diminished. This resource is available for non-commercial scientific use via our web site.
